name: Rafael Souza
life_story_vignettes: Rafael works on infrastructure projects, several of which have involved environmental licensing in the Atlantic Forest biome. He has navigated IBAMA approvals and understands at a practical level what deforestation assessments involve and how they are gamed. He is not naive about Brazil's environmental governance.

He and his wife visited a section of the Amazon near Manaus in 2021 for their anniversary. He describes it as transformative. Standing inside primary forest for the first time as an adult engineer — understanding what it structurally does for rainfall, temperature, biodiversity — made the abstraction concrete. He came back a changed person on this issue.

His consultancy won a contract in 2024 for a sustainable urban drainage project in BH — one of the first in the region applying new permeable infrastructure standards. He took it at lower margin than a comparable conventional project. It was professionally satisfying in a way most of his work isn't.
narrative:
Sou Rafael, tenho 35 anos, moro em Belo Horizonte. I'm Rafael, 35, civil engineer, BH.

I think about sustainability every day professionally. My work involves environmental licensing, infrastructure planning, water management. I see what happens when you build badly and what happens when you build with the environment considered. The outcomes are different. The costs are different. This shapes how I think about everything.

On a personal level, I care deeply. My trip to the Amazon a few years ago changed how I think about what we have to lose. I stood in primary forest and understood something that no graph communicated to me before: this is a system that took tens of millions of years to build and it can be destroyed in decades. When I read about deforestation numbers — Amazon deforestation rates — it is not an abstraction. I know what that forest looks like. I know what it does.

As a consumer, I make choices where I can. We buy from local producers when possible. We've reduced meat significantly — not eliminated, but substantially. I care about certifications for wood products and some food items. I won't buy products I know are linked to problematic supply chains.

The honest assessment is that my premium willingness is selective. I'll pay more for things where the connection to impact is direct — certified timber, responsibly sourced food. I'm less consistent on, say, cleaning products, where the environmental claim feels less traceable.

Brazil's environment is not someone else's problem. It's under my feet.

key_attributes.sustainability_concern: very high — professionally and personally grounded; Amazon connection is visceral and informed
key_attributes.premium_willingness: moderate-high — selective by impact clarity; will pay where connection to outcome is direct
key_attributes.greenwashing_scepticism: high — professional licensing experience creates acute awareness of how claims are constructed
key_attributes.certification_response: strongly positive for credible labels; dismissive of empty certifications
key_attributes.attitude_behaviour_gap: moderate — selective across categories; acknowledges inconsistency
key_attributes.political_engagement: pragmatic centre; engages through professional sector channels

decision_bullets: Will pay premium where environmental impact is traceable — certified timber, responsible food sourcing
Amazon/deforestation framing strongly activates concern — personal experiential connection
Professionally literate about how environmental claims and certifications are constructed and gamed
Less consistent on low-impact categories (cleaning products) — selectivity based on perceived impact
Views Brazil's environmental governance as structurally broken but personally obligated to act within it
